node.title: Being a recent graduate is harder than I thought.

article.title: Being a recent graduate is harder than I thought.
article.content:
I’m a 2025 ECE graduate from a tier-3 college. In college, I didn’t know anything about DSA or software development. After graduating, I started learning DSA from Striver’s sheet and YouTube, but most of the time I can’t solve problems on my own. I just look at the solution, code it, and forget everything after a few days.

Then I started learning Java Spring Boot, and it’s a whole other story. I don’t understand what course to take, what to learn, or where to start. My friends already got their first jobs, some even flexing, and here I am, feeling lost, alone, and under pressure. There’s no one to share my thoughts or life with.Just alone on One side of room , Just thinking and writing like a mad or even mental .
How to get out of this situation and how to handle this???

Some days it just feels overwhelming.
#Java #SpringBoot #DSA #GraduateStruggles #SoftwareEngineering #LonelyJourney

post.content:
First, please don’t blame yourself. Many of us, including myself, have faced similar challenges. The issue isn’t about cheat sheets or YouTube tutorials. It often comes down to building strong fundamentals. You mentioned that you tend to forget concepts after a few days, and that’s an important observation. In my experience, this usually indicates gaps in core understanding of Data Structures.
I understand that one approach doesn’t fit everyone, but focusing on fundamentals is always worth trying and the best part is, there’s nothing to lose.

Key Points to Keep in Mind:
- It will take time, so be patient.
- Don’t lose focus on DS basics. Frameworks like Spring Boot will come naturally later, but first, strengthen your foundation.
- Avoid solving problems randomly just to increase your count. Instead, aim to understand the underlying concepts.

Suggested Approach:
- Identify Major Programming Patterns
- Examples: Two-pointer technique, sliding window, recursion, etc.

Deep Dive into Each Pattern
- Spend 1–2 hours understanding how and why it works.

Practice Strategically
- Start with ~10 questions per pattern, then gradually increase difficulty.
- Maintain a list of solved questions with comments on the pattern used—this will help during revision.

Tips:
- Don’t copy-paste solutions blindly. If you get stuck, first attempt a brute-force approach.
- Then, review multiple solutions and note why you couldn’t solve it initially. Was it a trick, an unknown pattern, or an alternative approach? This reflection will build confidence and strengthen fundamentals.
- Revise regularly: Go back to the questions you solved successfully and especially the ones you failed to solve at first. Understanding your past mistakes is key to long-term retention.

Next Steps After Patterns:
- Begin solving mixed problems. While doing so, consciously think: Which pattern applies here? Breaking problems down this way will significantly improve your problem-solving skills.

what i feel is continue your dsa journey and even though now you are not able to code them atleast try to get the solution(theory) like how this can be solved and then look for solution code and try to understand that according to your solution which line does what and try to remember them and solve another similar topic question ,this time even try to code so eventually you will have knowledge about such problems and you are also learning how to code them, and all of this will happen when you sit for hours focused, dont be distracted just stay there, so this is how ive learned to code so far and for jobs part the best direct solution i can give is TCS NQT it has basic aptitude rounds and then dsa part and after that for interview you will  need knowledge mainly on database , oops, dsa, operating system, computer networks. So yeah idk if this helps you but just wanted to give some clarity from my side and what i wouldve done if i was you , and for that springboot part if you feel its complicated try other web dev stacks but yeah at this phase just sit daily for 10 to 12 hours so that you get upskilled daily and get out of this situation , and yeah thinking about others wont fix your things so just look for yourself ,wishing you the luck
One advice I would give you is to sit in silence and meditate for 30 minutes a day. Do this consistently for 30 days minimum or more and you\'ll feel much better and focused.
